evidence: κT,Classical. This agreement is further discussed below. The LD-PWTE total thermal conductivity calculated using quantum statistics

paper_title: Exceptionally Enhanced Thermal Conductivity of Aluminum Driven by Extreme Pressures: A First-Principles Study
evidence: lattice contribution increases with application of pressure, shown by inset of Figure 3c for ~82 GPa

paper_title: Exceptionally Enhanced Thermal Conductivity of Aluminum Driven by Extreme Pressures: A First-Principles Study
evidence: inset of Figure 3c for ~82 GPa pressure showing temperature-dependent thermal conductivity

paper_title: Exceptionally Enhanced Thermal Conductivity of Aluminum Driven by Extreme Pressures: A First-Principles Study
condition: 207 GPa pressure, room temperature, ~8x enhancement from ambient
evidence: thermal conductivity of Al at 207 GPa is ~8x higher than that at ambient pressure

paper_title: Exceptionally Enhanced Thermal Conductivity of Aluminum Driven by Extreme Pressures: A First-Principles Study
condition: 200 GPa pressure, 100 K, lattice contribution ~50% of total
evidence: contribution from lattice thermal conductivity is further enhanced to ~50% of total at 100 K under 200 GPa

paper_title: Exceptionally Enhanced Thermal Conductivity of Aluminum Driven by Extreme Pressures: A First-Principles Study
condition: 200 GPa pressure, 300 K, lattice contribution ~20% of total
evidence: lattice contributions culminate to ~20% of total thermal conductivity at room temperature for 200 GPa

paper_title: Exceptionally Enhanced Thermal Conductivity of Aluminum Driven by Extreme Pressures: A First-Principles Study
condition: ambient pressure, 300 K, lattice contribution ~5% of total
evidence: lattice contribution ~5% at ambient conditions versus ~20% at 200 GPa

paper_title: Exceptionally Enhanced Thermal Conductivity of Aluminum Driven by Extreme Pressures: A First-Principles Study
condition: ambient pressure, electronic thermal conductivity contribution
evidence: electrons are the dominant heat carriers at relatively higher temperatures

paper_title: Thermal Conductivity of Gold and Silver at High Pressures
evidence: average absolute values of a at atmospheric pressure and at T=315 K for the unannealed specimens were a=(1.30±0.02) cm2 s-1 for gold

paper_title: Thermal Conductivity of Gold and Silver at High Pressures
evidence: average absolute values of a at atmospheric pressure and at T=315 K for the unannealed specimens were a=(1.75±0.05) cm2 s-1 for silver

paper_title: Thermal Conductivity of Gold and Silver at High Pressures
evidence: For the annealed specimens the corresponding values were found to be a=1.33 cm2 s-1 for gold

paper_title: Thermal Conductivity of Gold and Silver at High Pressures
evidence: For the annealed specimens the corresponding values were found to be a=1.81 cm2 s-1 for silver

paper_title: Non-monotonic pressure dependence of the thermal conductivity of boron arsenide
evidence: κ calculations for κ of MgO with natural isotopic mix is in reasonable quantitative agreement with experimental data

paper_title: Non-monotonic pressure dependence of the thermal conductivity of boron arsenide
evidence: κ indeed increases monotonically with pressure in Fig. 1b, c, respectively, in-line with conventional expectation

paper_title: Non-monotonic pressure dependence of the thermal conductivity of boron arsenide
evidence: both isotopically pure BSb and BSb with natural isotopic mix show κ(3+4) peak in pressure dependence
